skill.name: gitops-workflow
skill.description: Guide GitOps workflows for Kubernetes with Argo CD and Flux, including installation, repository layout, sync policy design, progressive delivery, and secret management.
skill.summary: Plan and review GitOps delivery with Argo CD, Flux, and Kubernetes.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command, network, sudo, and weak-crypto patterns. Review found these are Markdown GitOps examples, not executable skill code, with no evidence of prompt injection, malware, or data exfiltration. The skill still needs a publication warning because some examples can modify Kubernetes clusters, pass credentials on command lines, or run a network installer with sudo.

medium_findings.title: Remote Installer Runs With Sudo
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The Flux installation example pipes a script from a network URL directly into sudo bash. This is a real supply-chain and privilege-escalation risk if executed without verification, although it appears to be standard installation documentation rather than malicious code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact command combines curl, a public install URL, and sudo bash on one line. Confidence is high because this pattern would execute remote code with elevated privileges if copied or run by an agent.

medium_findings.title: Cluster-Mutating Commands In Examples
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The guide includes kubectl, helm, argocd, flux, and kubeseal commands that can create namespaces, install controllers, sync applications, prune resources, or alter cluster state. These examples are legitimate for a GitOps skill, but an agent should not execute them without explicit user approval and cluster context.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files show concrete operational commands in Markdown code blocks. The risk depends on execution context, so confidence is high for operational impact but not for malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Token Passed As Command-Line Argument
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The Argo CD repository example passes a password or token directly on the command line. This can expose credentials through shell history, process listings, or logs if used with a real secret.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example explicitly uses --password token in a CLI command. It is a placeholder, but the pattern is unsafe when replaced with a real credential.

medium_findings.title: Destructive Sync And Prune Options
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The examples enable prune, self-heal, force sync, and automated reconciliation. These are valid GitOps controls, but misconfiguration can delete resources or overwrite emergency manual changes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented settings are visible and can affect live cluster resources. They are normal GitOps features, so this is an operational safety concern rather than evidence of abuse.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URLs
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill contains public Argo CD, Flux, GitHub, and Kubernetes service URLs. They are expected in setup examples, but they can trigger network access or fetch mutable upstream manifests if executed directly.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are directly present in installation and configuration examples. They point to expected project or placeholder endpoints, so the concern is low unless commands are executed blindly.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The high static weak-cryptography alerts do not correspond to cryptographic algorithms or crypto API use. The cited lines contain GitOps principles, destination fields, service URLs, or placeholder configuration text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or cryptographic API usage at the cited locations. The scanner likely matched substrings inside ordinary YAML or prose.

low_findings.title: External Command Alerts Are Mostly Documentation
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Most Ruby or shell backtick alerts are caused by Markdown code fences and inline code formatting, not Ruby execution. The files are guidance documents and contain no script wrapper that automatically runs these snippets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command text is inside Markdown fences and no executable Ruby or shell script file was present among the audited files. The residual risk is from user or agent execution, not hidden code execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: curl Piped To sudo bash
dangerous_patterns.description: The installer example downloads a shell script from the network and executes it with elevated privileges. Require checksum or signature verification and explicit user approval before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The risky command form is present exactly as a single-line pipeline. This is a recognized dangerous pattern even when used for a legitimate tool installer.

dangerous_patterns.title: CLI Secret Argument
dangerous_patterns.description: The repository setup example uses a token-like password argument. Prefer secret prompts, environment variables managed outside logs, or credential stores.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The placeholder token is directly passed through --password. The exact exposure depends on how a user substitutes real credentials.

dangerous_patterns.title: Force And Prune Operations
dangerous_patterns.description: The sync examples include prune and force behavior that can delete or overwrite cluster resources. Use dry runs, diffs, approvals, and environment scoping before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly include pruning and forced sync operations. These are useful but can be destructive when aimed at the wrong cluster or namespace.

content.value_statement: Kubernetes teams often struggle to keep deployment state, approvals, and cluster drift aligned across environments. This skill provides structured GitOps guidance for Argo CD and Flux workflows, including setup, sync policies, progressive delivery, and secret handling.
actual_capabilities: Explains Argo CD and Flux setup patterns for Kubernetes clusters.
Defines GitOps repository layouts for applications, infrastructure, and environments.
Provides examples for automated sync, manual sync, retries, windows, and health checks.
Covers progressive delivery patterns such as canary and blue-green releases.
Describes secret management options using External Secrets and Sealed Secrets.
Lists troubleshooting commands for sync failures and drift investigation.
limitations: It is guidance content and does not validate a live cluster automatically.
Examples use placeholder repositories, domains, namespaces, and credentials.
Some commands can change production resources if executed without review.
It does not replace vendor documentation or organization-specific release policy.

use_cases.title: Bootstrap a GitOps platform
use_cases.description: Plan a first Argo CD or Flux installation with repository structure, controller setup, and environment paths.

use_cases.title: Tune deployment reconciliation
use_cases.description: Compare automated sync, manual sync, retry policies, sync windows, and prune behavior before rollout.

use_cases.title: Standardize release governance
use_cases.description: Document safe deployment practices for approvals, secrets, staging validation, and production promotion.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a GitOps tool
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner evaluation of Argo CD and Flux options.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose between Argo CD and Flux for a Kubernetes GitOps rollout. Compare setup effort, sync behavior, access control, and operational fit.

prompt_templates.title: Design repository layout
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate planning for environments and application ownership.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a GitOps repository layout for staging and production. Include application folders, infrastructure folders, environment boundaries, and review gates.

prompt_templates.title: Review sync policy safety
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of reconciliation settings before production use.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this GitOps sync policy for production risk. Focus on prune, self-heal, force behavior, sync windows, retries, and rollback safety.

prompt_templates.title: Plan multi-cluster rollout
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced rollout planning across teams and clusters.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a multi-cluster GitOps rollout plan using Argo CD or Flux. Include bootstrap steps, RBAC, secret handling, progressive delivery, monitoring, and failure recovery.

output_examples.input: Plan GitOps for a new production service.
output_examples.output: A structured rollout plan covering repository layout, controller choice, namespace strategy, approval gates, sync policy, secrets, and monitoring checks.

output_examples.input: Review an auto-sync policy before enabling it.
output: Risk notes for prune and self-heal settings.
Recommended safeguards such as staging tests, sync windows, and manual approval.
Operational checks for rollback and alerts.

output_examples.output: A diagnostic path that checks desired state, controller status, diff output, health checks, permissions, and recent Git changes.

best_practices: Review cluster context, namespace, and target repository before running any command.
Use staged promotion, approvals, and sync windows for production changes.
Keep real secrets out of Git and avoid passing tokens directly on command lines.
anti_patterns: Do not run installer pipelines with elevated privileges without verification.
Do not enable prune or force sync in production before testing rollback behavior.
Do not copy placeholder repository URLs, domains, or tokens into live configuration.

faq.question: Does this skill install Argo CD or Flux automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and example commands. Users should review commands before running them.

faq.question: Can it help choose between Argo CD and Flux?
faq.answer: Yes. It can compare workflow fit, setup style, reconciliation behavior, and team operating needs.

faq.question: Does it cover production safety controls?
faq.answer: Yes. It discusses approvals, RBAC, sync windows, health checks, alerts, and staging validation.

faq.question: Does it manage secrets directly?
faq.answer: No. It describes patterns such as External Secrets and Sealed Secrets, but does not access secret stores.

faq.question: Can the examples affect a live cluster?
faq.answer: Yes, if a user runs them. Some examples create resources, install controllers, or sync applications.

faq.question: Is this suitable for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The content is compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code for planning and review workflows.
